2604.11312.title: Network Effects and Agreement Drift in LLM Debates
2604.11312.paper_summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated an unprecedented ability to simulate human-like social behaviors, making them useful tools for simulating complex social systems. However, it remains unclear to what extent these simulations can be trusted to accurately capture key social mechanisms, particularly in highly unbalanced contexts involving minority groups. This paper uses a network generation model with controlled homophily and class sizes to examine how LLM agents behave collectively in multi-round debates. Moreover, our findings highlight a particular directional susceptibility that we term \textit{agreement drift}, in which agents are more likely to shift toward specific positions on the opinion scale. Overall, our findings highlight the need to disentangle structural effects from model biases before treating LLM populations as behavioral proxies for human groups.
2604.11312.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型（LLMs）展示了前所未有的能力來模擬類似人類的社會行為，使其成為模擬複雜社會系統的有用工具。<lb/>然而，目前尚不清楚這些模擬在多大程度上可以被信任，以準確捕捉關鍵的社會機制，特別是在涉及少數群體的高度不平衡情境中。<lb/>本文使用一個具有控制同質性和班級規模的網絡生成模型，來檢視LLM代理在多輪辯論中的集體行為。<lb/>此外，我們的研究結果突顯了一種特殊的方向性易感性，我們稱之為\textit{協議漂移}，在這種情況下，代理更有可能向意見尺度上的特定立場轉變。<lb/>總體而言，我們的研究結果強調在將LLM群體視為人類群體的行為代理之前，需要將結構效應與模型偏見分開。
2604.11312.author: Erica Cau et.al.
2604.11312.authors: Erica Cau, Andrea Failla, Giulio Rossetti
